flashcards.back: Multiple processors share access to a single, common address space (global memory). All processors can read and write to the same memory locations.
Each processor has its own private memory and processors communicate by passing messages over an interconnection network. There is no global address space.

flashcards.front: What is the primary programming model for shared-memory systems?
flashcards.back: Multithreading using APIs like OpenMP or Pthreads, where threads share variables in a common address space.

flashcards.front: What is the primary programming model for distributed-memory systems?
flashcards.back: Message Passing using libraries like MPI (Message Passing Interface), where processes explicitly send and receive data.

flashcards.front: What is the key scalability limitation of shared-memory systems?
flashcards.back: Memory and bus bandwidth become a bottleneck as the number of processors increases, limiting practical scaling to tens or hundreds of cores.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between UMA and NUMA in shared-memory systems?
flashcards.back: UMA (Uniform Memory Access): All processors have equal access time to all memory. NUMA (Non-Uniform Memory Access): Access time depends on memory location relative to the processor -- local memory is faster than remote memory.

flashcards.front: Why do shared-memory systems need cache coherence protocols?
flashcards.back: When multiple processors cache copies of the same data, a write by one processor can make other caches stale. Cache coherence protocols (e.g., MESI) ensure all caches see a consistent view of shared data.

flashcards.front: What is a hybrid parallel architecture?
flashcards.back: A system that combines both shared and distributed memory. Typically a cluster of nodes (distributed memory between nodes) where each node is a multi-core shared-memory machine. Programmed with MPI + OpenMP.

flashcards.front: Why is distributed-memory programming considered more difficult?
flashcards.back: The programmer must manually decompose data, distribute it across nodes, and explicitly code all send/receive communication. There is no implicit data sharing -- every data exchange must be programmed.

flashcards.front: What is the cost advantage of distributed-memory systems?
flashcards.back: Distributed-memory clusters can be built from commodity off-the-shelf hardware (standard servers connected by a network), making them much more cost-effective than large coherent shared-memory machines.
